screens.prompt: Take any integer, square it, then divide by 3. Can the remainder ever be 2?
choices: Yes, for some integers the remainder is 2
No, squares leave remainder 0 or 1 when divided by 3, never 2
screens.reveal: Hold that thought. By the end of this lesson you'll be able to prove this exactly, by checking every possible case.

screens.prompt: Proof by exhaustion is a genuinely valid proof provided...

choices.text: every possible case is included in the list you check, with none left out

choices.feedback: The number of examples doesn't matter on its own. What matters is whether the cases you checked are the complete set the statement covers.

choices.text: the statement covers infinitely many values
choices.feedback: Exhaustion needs a finite, genuinely complete list of cases. An infinite, unrestricted set isn't exhaustible this way; deduction is the right tool for that instead.

screens.successFeedback: Right. Exhaustion is only as strong as its case list is complete.

screens.prompt: Claim: $n^2+n$ is even for every integer $0 \le n \le 4$. Check the case $n=3$: what is $3^2+3$?
Now check the last case, $n=4$: what is $4^2+4$?
screens.successFeedback: 20, and that's the last of the five cases $n=0,1,2,3,4$, all even, so the claim is proved by exhaustion for this range. But be careful: this only proves the claim for $0 \le n \le 4$. It doesn't prove $n^2+n$ is even for every integer; that would need the deduction from the previous lesson, since $n^2+n=n(n+1)$ is a product of consecutive integers.

screens.prompt: Match each remainder case for $n$ to the remainder $n^2$ leaves when divided by 3.
Every integer is $3k$, $3k+1$, or $3k+2$ for some integer $k$. Why does checking just these three cases prove a result for every integer, not just three numbers?

choices.text: Because every integer falls into exactly one of these three categories; there's no fourth option, so the three cases are genuinely exhaustive
Because 3 is a small number, so 3 cases are always enough
choices.feedback: How many cases are needed depends on genuinely covering every possibility, not on the number being small. It happens to be 3 here only because remainders mod 3 take exactly 3 values.

$k$ ranging over all integers is true, but that's the job of the algebra inside each case. The three cases themselves must be complete, which they are, because every integer has exactly one of three remainders mod 3.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly. The three remainder classes are the only options for any integer, which is what makes the case list genuinely exhaustive.

screens.body:
Every integer falls into exactly one of three cases: $n=3k$, $n=3k+1$, or $n=3k+2$, for some integer $k$. Check each:

$$
(3k)^2 = 9k^2 = 3(3k^2) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{remainder } 0.
$$

$$
(3k+1)^2 = 9k^2+6k+1 = 3(3k^2+2k)+1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{remainder } 1.
$$

$$
(3k+2)^2 = 9k^2+12k+4 = 3(3k^2+4k+1)+1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{remainder } 1.
$$

These three cases are the only possibilities for any integer, so the claim is proved for every integer $n$.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: This argument mixes exhaustion (three cases) with deduction (algebra using $k$) inside each case. Why is that combination needed to cover every integer, rather than exhaustion on its own?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: There are infinitely many integers, so we can't exhaust them one at a time. Exhaustion covers the three remainder categories, which really are the only options, while the algebra with $k$ inside each category covers every integer belonging to that category at once. Deduction handles the infinite part; exhaustion handles the finite part.

screens.worked: Every integer is either even ($n=2k$) or odd ($n=2k+1$), for some integer $k$. Case $n=2k$: $n^2 = 4k^2$, which is even. Case $n=2k+1$: $n^2 = 4k^2+4k+1$, which is one more than a multiple of 4, hence odd.
screens.prompt: So in both cases, $n^2$ has the ___ parity as $n$ itself. What single word goes in the blank?
hints: Compare: when $n$ is even, $n^2$ came out even; when $n$ is odd, $n^2$ came out odd.
screens.successFeedback: Same. $n^2$ always keeps the same parity as $n$, and the two cases (even, odd) are a genuinely exhaustive split: there's no third option for an integer. You predicted squares mod 3 can never leave remainder 2, and the full case check above proves it for every integer at once, not just the ones you tried.
